summary: In the 1960s, the civil rights movement was sweeping through the South, including Fredericksburg. We participated in sit-ins and picket lines, organizing boycotts against chain stores to make an economic impact. It was a tough time without modern conveniences like phones or cars, but we were inspired by leaders like Martin Luther King Jr. and James Farmer. The movement was intense but brief in Fredericksburg, lasting only a few months in 1960.

I grew up in Fredericksburg, Virginia, where much of the civil rights activity took place in the 1960s. We held mass meetings in local churches and organized sit-ins at downtown chain stores like Woolworth, Grant's, and Newberry's. The Colonial Theatre and Victoria Theatre were important local places where segregation was challenged. It was a small town, but the movement here was connected to larger Southern struggles.

Harry Belafonte was a prominent figure in the civil rights movement, and I remember when his wife at the time came down to Fredericksburg to speak at a mass meeting. Although we didn't know him personally, his presence and activism were well known and inspiring to us. His involvement helped bring attention and energy to our local efforts during the movement.

Martin Luther King Jr. was the spiritual leader of the civil rights movement and someone we learned about through television and mass meetings. His words and leadership moved people deeply, even in Fredericksburg where we never met him personally. He inspired us to remain peaceful and steadfast during the sit-ins and picket lines in 1960.

Whitney Young was known as the economic leader of the civil rights movement, working through the Urban League. While we didn't have direct contact with him, his role was important in shaping the economic strategies behind our local protests. His leadership helped guide efforts to create financial pressure on segregated businesses.

The Urban League was a key organization during the civil rights movement, focusing on economic issues. Whitney Young led the Urban League, and although I wasn't directly involved with the organization, their work influenced our local activism in Fredericksburg. They helped frame the economic strategies that we used in boycotting chain stores.

Roy Wilkins was the legal leader of the civil rights movement through his work with the NAACP. We knew of him as one of the major figures guiding the legal battles for civil rights, even though we never met him personally. His leadership helped support the local NAACP chapter in Fredericksburg where my mother was a youth advisor.

entity: National Association for the Advancement of Colored People
summary: The NAACP was very active in Fredericksburg during the civil rights movement, with a strong local chapter led by Dr. Philip Wyatt. My mother served as one of the youth advisors, helping to organize young people for sit-ins and picket lines. The NAACP provided structure and guidance for peaceful protests and learning sessions that prepared us for the challenges ahead.

James Farmer was a legendary activist known for spearheading the Freedom Rides and demonstrations across the South. Though we didn't know him personally in Fredericksburg, his activism inspired us greatly. He represented the direct action and protest side of the civil rights movement that we were part of locally.

The Freedom Rides were a series of protests against segregation in the South, led by activists like James Farmer. While I didn't participate directly, the Freedom Rides inspired our local efforts in Fredericksburg to challenge segregation through sit-ins and picket lines. They were a symbol of courage and direct action during the civil rights movement.

The Birmingham campaign influenced our tactics in Fredericksburg, especially the use of young people in sit-ins and picket lines. We learned from the struggles there that young people were less likely to lose jobs and could be more effective in protests. It was a model that helped shape our local civil rights activities in 1960.

entity: North Carolina Agricultural and Technical State University
summary: North Carolina A&T University was where the sit-in movement started, led by college students. Their courageous actions inspired us in Fredericksburg to organize our own sit-ins and protests. The university became a symbol of youth-led activism in the civil rights movement.

Dr. Philip Wyatt was a well-known dentist and the leader of the NAACP in Fredericksburg during the civil rights movement. He was very active at the state level and helped organize local efforts. His leadership was crucial in guiding the youth and the community through the sit-ins and boycotts in 1960.

John Scott's mother, Mamie Scott, worked alongside my mother as a youth leader during the civil rights movement in Fredericksburg. Together, they helped organize young people for sit-ins and picket lines. The Scott family was part of the local leadership that made the movement possible in our town.

Woolworth was one of the main chain stores in downtown Fredericksburg where we organized sit-ins and boycotts in 1960. It was a popular place with a lunch counter that was segregated, so it became a target for our protests. The store is now an antique mall, but back then it was a key battleground in the civil rights movement locally.

Grant's was another chain store downtown Fredericksburg where we held sit-ins and picket lines during the civil rights movement. It was a busy place where people ate lunch, so boycotting it was meant to hit them economically. Grant's location is now where Ben Franklin is, but back then it was central to our protests.

Newberry's was one of the four main chain stores in Fredericksburg targeted during the 1960 sit-ins and boycotts. Located in the 900 block of Caroline Street, it had a lunch counter that was segregated. Our efforts there were part of a coordinated economic strategy to challenge segregation across the South.

People's Drug Store was a downtown Fredericksburg chain store with a segregated lunch counter that never changed its policy despite our protests. The manager, Mr. Southworth, was particularly hostile and refused to serve black customers while I was around. It was the only one of the four main stores that resisted integration during the civil rights movement.

During the segregated movie theater days in Fredericksburg, I used to buy Jujubes candy to throw down on the white folks from the balcony. It was a small act of rebellion and something I did for fun, even though it wasn't something to be proud of. Jujubes were a cheap hard candy that we enjoyed back then.

Good & Plenty was another type of hard candy that I used to buy and throw down from the segregated balconies in the Fredericksburg movie theaters. Like Jujubes, it was inexpensive and part of my small acts of mischief during those times. These candies were part of the childhood memories connected to segregation.

The Colonial Theatre on Caroline Street was a segregated movie theater where black patrons had to sit in the balcony and were given stale popcorn. It is now where Tea Tyme is located. I remember going there on Saturdays for cowboy movies and joining fan clubs for Roy Rogers and Hopalong Cassidy. One time, I was called to come downstairs for a prize, which made me feel like I was representing my whole race.

Tea Tyme is the current establishment located where the Colonial Theatre used to be on Caroline Street in Fredericksburg. Back in the 1960s, the Colonial Theatre was a segregated space where we sat in the balcony. It's interesting to see how the place has changed from a movie theater to a tea shop over the years.

Caroline Street was the main downtown area in Fredericksburg where many of the chain stores like Woolworth, Newberry's, and the Colonial Theatre were located. It was the center of our civil rights protests, including sit-ins and boycotts in 1960. The street holds many memories of struggle and change during that time.

Roy Rogers was one of the cowboy stars featured in Saturday movies at the Colonial Theatre in Fredericksburg. We could join his fan club by collecting little stubs, and I remember being part of that as a kid. These movies were a big part of our entertainment, even though we had to sit in the segregated balcony.

Hopalong Cassidy was another cowboy hero whose movies were shown on Saturdays at the Colonial Theatre in Fredericksburg. Like Roy Rogers, we could join his fan club, and it was a popular pastime for us kids. These movies were a rare chance to enjoy air conditioning despite the segregation.

Gene Autry was mentioned as one of the cowboy stars from the movies shown at the Colonial Theatre, though I don't remember him as clearly as others like Roy Rogers or Hopalong Cassidy. These cowboy films were a big part of our childhood entertainment in segregated Fredericksburg. They brought some joy despite the difficult times.

Tom Mix was one of the cowboy stars featured in the Saturday movies at the Colonial Theatre in Fredericksburg. I remember having a fan club card for him and going upstairs to watch the films from the balcony. These movies were a highlight of my youth, even though segregation limited where we could sit.

Lash LaRue was another cowboy star whose movies were shown at the Colonial Theatre in Fredericksburg. Like Tom Mix and Roy Rogers, he was part of the Saturday movie lineup that we kids enjoyed from the segregated balcony. These films were a big part of our entertainment during the civil rights era.

The Victoria Theatre in Fredericksburg was another segregated movie theater where black patrons had to sit in the balcony. It is now part of the Fredericksburg Baptist Church. I remember going there as a big kid, and it was one of the few places with air conditioning where we could escape the summer heat, even though we were separated from white audiences.
